mxCell.value: company Network Security — Traffic Flow Validation
Path-by-path validation that source → destination flows traverse the correct enforcement points without DPI duplication.

This pack accompanies the company Network Security Strategy (DOC-IT-IS-NSS-001) and validates that the tiered enforcement model holds end-to-end across every meaningful source → destination pair on the company network. It extends the three reference flows in the strategy doc (Flow 1/2/3) to cover the full set of paths so reviewers can confirm the restrictions, flows, and inspection points fit before the standard is circulated to the wider Network/Cloud/InfoSec teams.

• Page 2 — Master Flow Matrix. Single-page summary of every flow versus every enforcement point. Use for review/sign-off. • Pages 3–12 — One detailed path diagram per flow (F1–F10). Each page shows source → enforcement points → destination, what is inspected at each hop, a status banner (compliant / transitional / exception), and explanatory notes. • Status banners: ✅ green = compliant with the tiered model · ⚠️ amber = transitional, exception-based, or migration-dependent. • Cross-references at the bottom of each diagram point to the relevant standard / strategy section.

• F1 — Warehouse → WMS in GCVE • F2 — Warehouse → App in Native GCE • F3 — Warehouse → Legacy App in TSG DC • F4 — Warehouse → Internet / SaaS • F5 — Site to Site (exception) • F6 — Remote User (VPN today / ZTNA future) • F7 — Internet Inbound → DMZ • F8 — 3rd Party → company Service • F9 — Intra-Cloud (GCVE → GCE) • F10 — Other Cloud (Azure/AWS/Oracle) → company

1. Is the F3 (TSG DC) DPI duplication acceptable as transitional, and what is the target retirement date? 2. F6 — agree on ZTNA pilot scope and the criteria for declaring VPN concentrators ready for retirement. 3. F10 — confirm there are no current site-terminated private cloud links that would breach the standard. 4. Are there any flows missing from this set that the network/cloud team needs validated?
Master Flow Matrix — Source → Destination by Enforcement Point
Single source of truth for which inspection occurs where. 'Primary' = enforcement point of record. Rows correspond to detailed flow pages F1–F10.
⚠️ Transitional — DPI duplicated until DC retires
⚠️ Exception only — eliminate via cloud-mediated access
⚠️ Dual path — VPN today, ZTNA target
✅ Compliant — no hub backhaul; watch migration translation
✅ Compliant — termination at hub/DC, not at site

LEGEND PRIMARY = enforcement point of record · '—' = not in path · BYPASSED = intentionally not traversed · Inspection: SPI = stateful packet inspection · DPI = deep packet inspection · IPS = intrusion prevention · AM = anti-malware · SWG = secure web gateway · DLP = data-loss prevention · ZTNA = zero-trust network access. Each row maps to a detailed path diagram on the following pages (F1–F10). Cross-references: company Network Security Strategy DOC-IT-IS-NSS-001 §Enforcement Point Matrix; Network Segmentation Standard v2.0 DOC-IT-IS-NSS-002; Inspection & Threat Protection Standard DOC-IT-IS-ITP-001 §Inspection Placement Requirements.
Flow 1: Warehouse User → WMS in GCVE
Reference flow from Network Security Strategy. Three inspection points, no DPI duplication.

Site Firewall (Tier 1)
SD-WAN / MPLS Stateful ACL only Permit DC/Cloud

Hub Firewall — Equinix (Tier 2)
DPI + IPS/IDS Anti-Malware Threat Intel feeds

GCVE NSX DFW (Tier 3)
Micro-segmentation Web → App → DB rules No DPI re-inspection
✅ COMPLIANT — inspect once at hub, micro-seg at cloud, no DPI duplication.

NOTES
Inspection points: 3 (SPI at site, DPI/IPS at hub, micro-seg in cloud). Single deep inspection point on the path — no duplication. Latency budget: <1ms site + 5–15ms hub + <1ms NSX = within WMS tolerance. This is the canonical site-to-cloud flow. All other warehouse→cloud flows follow this shape.

diagram.name: F2 — Warehouse → App in Native GCE

mxCell.value: Flow 2: Warehouse User → App in Native GCE
Same shape as F1, but enforcement at cloud shifts from NSX DFW to GCE VPC firewall + tags.

Site Firewall (Tier 1)
Stateful ACL Permit DC/Cloud

Hub Firewall — Equinix (Tier 2)
DPI + IPS/IDS Anti-Malware

GCE VPC Firewall (Tier 3)
Tag-based rules Security groups (translated from NSX)
⚠️ COMPLIANT — but GCVE→GCE migration MUST translate NSX rules to VPC rules (mandatory runbook step).

NOTES
GCVE→GCE migration risk: enforcement mechanism changes (NSX DFW → VPC firewall) but the security policy must NOT change. The application's communication matrix must be translated from NSX rules to VPC firewall rules + tags as a mandatory step in the migration runbook. Without this step there is a real risk of permissive default rules in GCE.

diagram.name: F3 — Warehouse → Legacy App in TSG DC

mxCell.value: Flow 3: Warehouse User → Legacy App in TSG Data Centre
Transitional path — workloads migrating off TSG DC to GCVE/GCE.

Site Firewall (Tier 1)
Stateful ACL Permit DC range

Hub Firewall — Equinix (Tier 2)
DPI + IPS/IDS Anti-Malware

TSG DC Firewall
SPI + DPI (legacy controls)
⚠️ TRANSITIONAL — DPI duplicated at hub + DC. Acceptable short-term; remove as workloads migrate to cloud.

NOTES
DPI is performed at BOTH the hub and the TSG DC firewall — this is the one DPI duplication in the current model. Justified short-term as the DC firewall is the legacy enforcement point and will be retired as workloads migrate to cloud. As migrations complete, the DC firewall's DPI/IPS role should be deprecated and the hub becomes the single deep inspection point.
Flow 4: Warehouse User → Internet / SaaS via Zscaler
Internet-bound traffic does NOT traverse the hub. Single inspection at SSE.

Site Firewall (Tier 1)
Stateful ACL Permit Zscaler tunnel

Zscaler SSE (Tier 0)
SWG + URL filter SSL inspect + DLP Malware scan
✅ COMPLIANT — single inspection at SSE. Hub bypass intentional. Foundation for SASE.

NOTES
The hub firewall is NOT involved in this path. Internet-bound traffic is tunnelled directly from the site to Zscaler. This eliminates hub backhaul latency for the largest traffic class (internet/SaaS) and is the foundation for future SASE evolution. Hub IPS does not need to duplicate URL filtering or web malware scanning.
Denied by default. Where a business exception exists, traffic still inspected at hub.

Site A Firewall (Tier 1)
Stateful ACL Exception rule

Hub Firewall — Equinix (Tier 2)
DPI + IPS/IDS Anti-Malware on cross-site flow

Site B Firewall (Tier 1)
Stateful ACL Permit from Site A
⚠️ EXCEPTION-BASED — eliminate over time by moving shared apps to cloud. Audit all exceptions for expiry.

NOTES
Default rule: RFC 1918 deny between sites. This flow only applies where a documented business exception exists (e.g., shared WMS across warehouses). All exceptions must have an expiry date. Strategic goal: eliminate site-to-site exceptions over time by routing cross-site application access through the cloud (GCVE/GCE) rather than direct site-to-site, removing east-west spread risk.

diagram.name: F6 — Remote User (VPN today / ZTNA future)

mxCell.value: Flow 6: Remote / Mobile User → Internal App
Two paths shown: current state (VPN concentrator) and target state (ZTNA via SSE).

Hub / DC Firewall
DPI + IPS/IDS Anti-Malware

Zscaler ZTNA (Tier 0 — target)
Identity-based access Per-app brokering No network-layer reach
⚠️ DUAL PATH — VPN today, ZTNA target. Migrate as ZPA pilot completes.

NOTES
Current state: VPN concentrator places the remote device on the network. Hub/DC firewall still inspects via DPI/IPS. Target state (medium-term): Zscaler ZTNA replaces the VPN — user accesses a specific app via an identity-brokered session, without network-layer reach. This is a key SSE roadmap milestone and removes the VPN concentrator as a perimeter device.
Only permitted inbound path. All inbound terminates in DMZ — never directly into internal zones.

Hub / DC Firewall
DPI + IPS/IDS Anti-Malware WAF (if web)

External DMZ (Security Level 50)
Layer-3 only No direct DB/internal reach
✅ COMPLIANT — DMZ termination enforced. Internet→Trusted is a Critical-severity exception.

NOTES
Per Network Segmentation Standard §Internet Zone Rules: all connectivity from the internet MUST terminate in the Internet Zone (DMZ). Internal/Trusted zones never accept direct internet ingress. Internet→Trusted/Secure/Isolated/3rd-Party = Critical exception severity. If the service is web-facing, WAF is required (separate standard / addendum).
Flow 8: 3rd Party Network → company Service (B2B circuit)
3rd Party = Security Level 30 (Risky). Dedicated zone per partner. Inspected at hub/DC.

Hub / DC Firewall
DPI + IPS/IDS Anti-Malware Strict zone rules
✅ COMPLIANT — strict per-partner zoning. Watch for 'any' rules (High severity exceptions).

NOTES
Each 3rd party gets a dedicated zone (no co-tenancy without explicit design + sign-off from all parties). Source/destination 'any' to/from 3rd Party zone = High severity exception. Where a third party cannot terminate at a company firewall, a separate 3rd Party Network must be created by exception to host the VPN termination point. Anti-malware is particularly important on this path per the Inspection & Threat Protection Standard.
Flow 9: Intra-Cloud — GCVE VM → GCE Service
No hub backhaul. NSX DFW egress + GCE VPC firewall ingress. No DPI re-inspection.

NSX DFW (egress)
Micro-seg permit App → Cloud SQL
✅ COMPLIANT — no hub backhaul. Watch GCVE→GCE migrations for rule translation.

NOTES
Intra-cloud traffic does NOT transit the Equinix hub — that would be unnecessary backhaul. NSX DFW handles egress micro-seg, GCE VPC firewall handles ingress. No DPI/IPS at this tier unless a specific compliance scenario requires it. When a GCVE workload migrates to native GCE, the inter-tier rules must be translated from NSX policy to VPC rules — this is the same migration concern flagged in F2.
Flow 10: Other Cloud Tenant → company (private link / VPN)
Must terminate at hub or DC — never at a warehouse or office site.
Hub / DC Termination (private link or VPN) NOT at site
✅ COMPLIANT — termination at hub/DC enforced. Site-terminated cloud links are forbidden.

NOTES
Per Network Segmentation Standard §Cloud Zones: all non-GCVE/GCE cloud tenants must terminate via private link or VPN into a company datacentre or hub. Private cloud connections MUST NOT terminate at a warehouse or office site — this would bypass the hub inspection point. Once terminated, the traffic is treated as inter-zone traffic and follows standard hub DPI/IPS.
